name: Java & DevOps · Advanced
description: Harder Java & DevOps for experienced hires — concurrency, JVM internals, Kubernetes, IaC.

questions.text: A mutable object is used as a HashMap key, then one of its fields changes after insertion. What happens?
options: The map quietly re-sorts all of its entries
It throws an exception the moment you change it
Nothing changes, because keys are copied on insert
You may no longer be able to retrieve the entry
questions.explanation: The key's hashCode changes, so it lands in a different bucket than where it was stored — the entry becomes effectively unreachable. Keys must be effectively immutable.

questions.text: Under high contention, why does ConcurrentHashMap usually outperform Collections.synchronizedMap?
options: It caches the whole map in the CPU registers
It locks finer-grained, so buckets stay concurrent
It is fully single-threaded and serialises every call
It skips locking entirely and never blocks
questions.explanation: synchronizedMap serialises every operation on one lock; ConcurrentHashMap locks per-bucket/segment, so unrelated updates proceed in parallel.

questions.text: For a double-checked-locking singleton to be correct, the instance field must be…
options: Declared volatile, so the instance is safely published
Declared static, and nothing more than that
Declared transient, so it is not serialised
Declared final, which fully fixes the visibility issue
questions.explanation: Without volatile another thread can observe a non-null but partially-constructed instance due to reordering.

questions.text: Two threads increment a shared 'volatile int' with i++. Is it safe?
options: Yes, since all int operations are atomic anyway
Only on 64-bit JVMs, never on 32-bit ones
No — volatile gives visibility, not atomicity
Yes, because volatile makes i++ fully atomic
questions.explanation: i++ is read-modify-write, three steps. volatile ensures visibility of the latest value but doesn't make the compound operation atomic.

questions.text: A fixed thread pool backed by an unbounded queue is hammered with tasks faster than it can process. Risk?
options: The queue grows until the JVM runs out of memory
Nothing; an unbounded queue is always safe
Tasks beyond capacity are silently dropped
Extra worker threads are spawned automatically as needed
questions.explanation: An unbounded queue accepts work indefinitely; without backpressure or a bounded queue, memory is exhausted.

questions.text: Integer a=127,b=127 gives a==b true, but a=128,b=128 gives a==b false. Why?
options: The value 128 overflows and wraps around
Values -128..127 are cached, so == compares one object
It is simply a long-standing bug in the JVM
Autoboxing simply picks the result at random each time
questions.explanation: The Integer cache reuses instances for -128..127. Always compare boxed values with equals, not ==.

questions.text: A static collection keeps accumulating entries that are never removed. This is a classic…
options: A deadlock between two competing threads
A useful performance optimisation for lookups
A memory leak — entries stay reachable forever
A race condition on the shared collection
questions.explanation: Static references live for the app's lifetime, so anything they hold can never be garbage-collected.

questions.text: In try-with-resources, if the body throws and close() also throws, what happens to the close() exception?
options: The close() error is suppressed onto the primary one
The two exceptions are simply thrown separately in turn
Both exceptions are lost and nothing propagates
The close() error silently replaces the original
questions.explanation: The primary exception propagates; the close() exception is added to its suppressed list so nothing is lost.

questions.text: Which set of properties makes a class properly immutable?
options: Final fields, no setters, and defensive copies of mutables
Simply marking every field private is enough
Only making the class itself final is required
Wrapping every getter method inside a synchronized block
questions.explanation: Immutability needs all of these; a leaked reference to an internal mutable field breaks it even if fields are final.

questions.text: A parallel stream appends results into a shared ArrayList. What's wrong?
options: It simply refuses to compile with a shared collection
ArrayList isn't thread-safe; concurrent adds corrupt it
Parallel streams are simply always slower here
Nothing; parallel streams handle this safely
questions.explanation: Side-effecting shared mutable state from parallel streams causes races; use collect() with a concurrent-safe collector or a reduction.

questions.text: Calling Optional.get() without checking presence is discouraged because…
options: It has been deprecated in every recent Java version
It is noticeably slower than calling orElse
It quietly returns null instead of a value
It throws when empty, defeating Optional's purpose
questions.explanation: The point of Optional is to force handling the empty case; blind get() reintroduces the null-style crash. Prefer orElse/ifPresent/map.

questions.text: You override equals() but not hashCode(), then store the object in a HashSet and look it up. Result?
options: It is always found regardless of what the hash code is
May not be found — equal objects can hash differently
The code fails to compile without both methods
The set silently becomes sorted by hash code
questions.explanation: Hash-based collections locate objects by hashCode first; violating the equals/hashCode contract breaks lookups.

questions.text: A Comparator inconsistent with equals is used in a TreeSet. Consequence?
options: Elements it deems equal are dropped or merged
The TreeSet throws as soon as you add an item
Sorting simply runs faster with fewer checks
Nothing changes; equals is used for identity
questions.explanation: Sorted sets/maps use the comparator (not equals) for identity, so 'compare == 0' elements collapse together.

questions.text: A ThreadLocal is set but never removed on a pooled thread. Risk?
options: Nothing, since thread pools clear ThreadLocals for you
A deadlock the next time the thread is reused
Leaked state carried into later tasks on that thread
The thread pool quietly shrinks by one thread
questions.explanation: Pool threads are reused, so a lingering ThreadLocal leaks data across tasks and can retain memory. Always remove() in a finally.

questions.text: Throwing a checked exception from inside a standard Stream map() lambda…
options: Is automatically converted to an unchecked one
Compiles fine and propagates it up the stream
Is silently swallowed and the element skipped
Won't compile — you must catch or wrap it there
questions.explanation: The built-in functional interfaces don't declare checked exceptions, so you must handle or wrap them inside the lambda.

questions.text: thenApply vs thenCompose on CompletableFuture — when do you need thenCompose?
options: thenCompose is only ever used for logging output
They are identical and interchangeable always
thenApply flattens nested futures automatically
thenCompose flattens a function returning a future
questions.explanation: thenCompose flattens a future-returning function (like flatMap); thenApply would give you a CompletableFuture<CompletableFuture<...>>.

questions.text: A pod sets cpu and memory requests equal to its limits. Its QoS class is…
options: Burstable, sitting between the other two classes
Guaranteed, so it is evicted last under pressure
Preemptible, meaning it can be stopped anytime
BestEffort, since it declares no resource needs
questions.explanation: requests == limits for all resources gives Guaranteed QoS, which is evicted last under pressure.

questions.text: Under node memory pressure, which pods does the kubelet evict first?
options: Guaranteed pods are always evicted first of all
The most recently started pods are evicted first
Pods are evicted in a completely random order
BestEffort first, then Burstable, then Guaranteed
questions.explanation: Eviction follows QoS: BestEffort first, Guaranteed last, so critical guaranteed workloads survive longest.

questions.text: What does a PodDisruptionBudget protect against?
options: Images that fail to pull from the registry
Nodes failing unexpectedly from hardware faults
Too many pods going down at once during drains
Invalid YAML being applied to the cluster
questions.explanation: A PDB caps how many replicas can be voluntarily taken down simultaneously, preserving availability during maintenance.

questions.text: maxUnavailable and maxSurge in a Deployment control…
options: The CPU and memory limits set on each pod
How frequently the health probes are executed
How many pods may be down or added during a rollout
The total number of replicas the app should run overall
questions.explanation: They tune rollout speed vs availability: surge adds temporary extra pods, unavailable allows some to be down.

questions.text: When is a StatefulSet the right choice over a Deployment?
options: When you need stable identities and per-pod storage
Only for batch jobs that run once and then finish
For any ordinary, stateless web application
Whenever you simply want to use less memory
questions.explanation: StatefulSets give ordered, stable pod identities and sticky volumes — needed by databases and clustered stores.

questions.text: A headless Service (clusterIP: None) provides…
options: Per-pod DNS records addressing pods individually
Automatic TLS termination for incoming traffic
A single virtual IP load-balancing the pods
External load balancing across the whole cluster
questions.explanation: Without a cluster IP, DNS returns each pod's address, which is how StatefulSet members find each other.

questions.text: Why does Terraform use state locking (e.g. via DynamoDB)?
options: Stores your secrets safely inside the state
Makes each plan run noticeably faster
Stops concurrent applies from corrupting the state
Encrypts the state file automatically while it is at rest
questions.explanation: A lock ensures only one apply mutates state at a time; concurrent writes could leave state inconsistent with reality.

questions.text: Why prefer for_each over count for a set of named resources in Terraform?
options: Stable keys, so removing one doesn't recreate others
count cannot create more than one resource
It runs plans and applies much faster than count does
It is simply the newer of the two features
questions.explanation: count addresses by position, so deleting the middle item shifts indices and forces recreation; for_each keys are stable.

questions.text: Why COPY package.json and install deps before COPY of the source in a Dockerfile?
options: It makes the base image considerably smaller
Docker requires dependencies to appear before any source
It is purely a matter of personal style
Layer caching — deps aren't reinstalled on code change
questions.explanation: Ordering stable steps first lets Docker reuse the cached dependency layer across builds where only code changed.

questions.text: A key advantage of a canary release over blue-green?
options: It removes the need for any monitoring at all
Gradual exposure limits blast radius, enables metrics
It switches all of the traffic across instantly at once
It needs no extra infrastructure to run at all
questions.explanation: Canary shifts a slice of traffic first so you can watch metrics and abort before everyone is affected.

questions.text: With at-least-once message delivery, consumers must be…
options: Idempotent, so duplicate deliveries do no harm
Strictly single-threaded to avoid any races
Completely stateless with no stored data
Always faster than the producers sending them
questions.explanation: At-least-once can redeliver, so processing the same message twice must not double-apply effects.

questions.text: A Horizontal Pod Autoscaler scales replicas based on…
options: The size on disk of the container image
The current time of day and day of week
The number of worker nodes present in the cluster
Observed metrics compared against a set target
questions.explanation: The HPA adjusts replica count to keep the measured metric near the target you set.

questions.text: TLS is terminated at the load balancer. Traffic from the LB to the backend is then…
options: Always encrypted end-to-end without extra work
Cached at the balancer for later reuse
Unencrypted onward unless you re-encrypt the hop
Automatically compressed before reaching backends
questions.explanation: Termination decrypts at the edge; the hop to the backend is plaintext unless you explicitly re-encrypt it.

questions.text: During a planned failover, a very high DNS TTL causes…
options: The cutover happens instantly for everyone
Clients keep the old IP until their caches expire
Nothing at all; TTL has no bearing whatsoever on failover
DNS resolution stops working altogether
questions.explanation: Caches honour the TTL, so a long TTL delays the switch. Reduce TTL ahead of a planned change.

questions.text: A pod's readiness probe fails but liveness passes. What happens?
options: Nothing changes; it keeps receiving traffic
It is deleted and rescheduled onto another node
It stays up but is pulled from Service endpoints
It is immediately restarted by the kubelet
questions.explanation: Readiness gates traffic without restarting; the pod stays up but is taken out of the load-balancing rotation.

questions.text: In immutable infrastructure, how do you 'fix' a misbehaving server?
options: Edit its configuration files directly in place and reload
Reboot it repeatedly until the fault clears
SSH in and patch the running server in place
Replace it with a fresh instance from a known image
questions.explanation: Immutable infra replaces rather than mutates, so servers stay reproducible and free of configuration drift.

questions.text: A non-idempotent deploy step is retried by CI after a timeout. Risk?
options: Nothing; the second attempt simply overwrites
Duplicate or partial side effects; make it idempotent
A faster deploy overall, since the retry runs in parallel
It rolls itself back to the previous version
questions.explanation: If the first attempt partly succeeded, a blind retry can double-apply; idempotency makes retries harmless.
